facts.context: When sociologists study the structure of layers in society and people's movement between them, they call this:
facts.truth: social stratification
distractors: social control
social conflict
social solidarity

facts.context: The four ideal types of social action that Weber identified were as follows: instrumentally-rational, value-rational, traditional, and...
facts.truth: affectual
distractors: affective
effective
infected

facts.context: Sullivan's (2000) study suggested that the proportion of housework men did was greatest when:
facts.truth: they were unemployed or both partners worked full time
distractors: they had rediscovered themselves as 'new men'
their wives were at home and nagged them all the time
exciting gadgets like the hoover and electric iron were invented

facts.context: The 'new man' of the 1980s was alleged to be:
facts.truth: sensitive, caring, and emotional
distractors: laddish, aggressive, and violent
a strong and dependable breadwinner
openly bisexual and proud of it

facts.context: The term 'over-urbanization' means that:
facts.truth: in poorer countries, the rapidly developing cities drain resources from the rural areas
distractors: life in modern Western cities is so far removed from that of the Third World that we find it difficult to understand these societies
the extent to which urbanization affects development has been exaggerated
governments are so preoccupied with urbanization in the West that they forget to attend to problems in the Third World

facts.context: According to Hakim (1995, 1996), the majority of part-time work is done by women because:
facts.truth: they make a rational choice to combine employment with their 'home-centred' lives
distractors: part-time jobs pay relatively high wages and so are financially beneficial
their choices are constrained by structural factors, such as being unable to afford childcare
full time work is not available in the kinds of occupations that they want to enter

facts.context: Patterns of drug use in Britain reveal that:
facts.truth: it has increased and is no longer confined to a hedonistic youth subculture
distractors: the most commonly used recreational drug is Ecstasy
teenage girls are more likely to experiment with hard drugs than teenage boys
despite a series of moral panics, the use of illegal drugs is very rare

facts.context: Murray thought that the 'underclass' consisted of people who:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: formed an inferior 'race' with low levels of intelligence
lived morally unsound lives of crime and squalor
were too reliant upon welfare benefits

facts.context: Religious organizations such as the Church of Norway, Islam, the Church of England, and the Church of Greece have which of the following characteristics in common?
facts.truth: They are monotheistic.
distractors: They are cultures as well as churches.
They practice separation of church and state.
They exclude women as clergy.

facts.context: A researcher mailed 1,000 questionnaires to members of a labor union and received 300 back. Most of those who returned the questionnaires indicated that they were dissatisfied with the union. Results based only on the returned questionnaires are most likely contaminated by
facts.truth: response rate bias
distractors: researcher bias
confusing correlation and causation
statistical regression

facts.context: Role-learning theory suggests that
facts.truth: we internalise and take on social roles from a pre-existing framework
distractors: we create and negotiate our roles through interaction with others
social roles are not fixed or stable but fluid and pluralistic
roles have to be learned to suppress unconscious motivations

facts.context: Which of the following best describes the practice of placing the blame for the problems of a society on one particular group?
facts.truth: Scapegoating
distractors: Ethnocentrism
Stereotyping
Institutionalization

facts.context: Which of the following is consistent with results of current research conducted in the area of gender roles and the workplace?
facts.truth: Most men married to working women do not share housework and childcare obligations equally with their working wives.
distractors: In about a quarter of households with children, men stay home with the children so their wives can work.
Most women believe that there is equal opportunity today between men and women in career choice, advancement, and income.
Most men believe that women should be homemakers because men earn more money.

facts.context: The term 'assets' refers to:
facts.truth: a stock of economic resources, including land, shares and bank deposits
distractors: the culturally valued commodities and standards of living that make the poor feel relatively deprived
the flow of money a person receives from their salary or wage
the 'slices' of the population who own differing amounts of wealth

facts.context: Sociologists like Bordo (1993) have attributed the 'epidemic' of anorexia in Western cultures to:
facts.truth: the new ideal of independent, self-assured femininity, combined with cultural values of achievement and control
distractors: a generally ascetic, frugal lifestyle that can be taken to extremes
traditional images of women as emotional, domesticated, and unassertive
the social construction of mental illness, through which 'normal' patterns of behaviour are pathologized

facts.context: In the 1990 reform of the National Health Service, hospitals became:
facts.truth: self-governing trusts competing for purchasing contracts from health authorities
distractors: state-controlled providers, dependent on funding from the central government
increasingly detached from health authorities and providers of private health care
less inclined to run themselves efficiently, as demand for health care was falling

facts.context: The term 'feminist standpoint' suggests:
facts.truth: studying society from the perspective of women
distractors: taking a stand on the issues neglected by feminism
the recognition of difference and diversity in women's lives
a tendency to ignore the gendered nature of knowledge

facts.context: World-affirming religions:
facts.truth: embrace conventional cultural values but offer new means of achieving them
distractors: react against the loss of any meaningful religious content in the teachings of churches
adopt an attitude of mild disapproval towards mainstream social values
reject both the goals and means of conventional society and provide utopian alternatives

facts.context: The 'extension pattern' that Parker (1976) identified was said to be:
facts.truth: characteristic of professionals and skilled workers, whose work interests spilled over into leisure time
distractors: the lifestyle of clerical and unskilled manual workers, who saw leisure time as a chance to escape from alienating work
a neutral balance between work and leisure as neither connected nor oppositional, as evidenced by semi-skilled manual and clerical workers
the tendency of students to leave essay-writing until the last minute and then ask for an extended deadline

facts.context: Smith & Tomlinson argued that:
facts.truth: school character far outweighed ethnic background in determining educational success
distractors: differences in ethnic background were more significant than class or gender in predicting educational achievement
racism in schools undermined the move towards equality of opportunity for children from ethnic minorites
gender differences in educational achievement could be attributed to inequalities of class

facts.context: A moral panic occurs when:
facts.truth: the media exaggerate reports of deviant groups, generating hostile reactions
distractors: children watch violent or sexually explicit films and then copy the behaviour
audiences challenge the ethnic stereotypes represented by the media
elitists express concern with the Americanization of culture

facts.context: Which of the following was not part of Weber's ideal type of bureaucracy?
facts.truth: extensive paperwork
distractors: impersonal rules
hierarchy of officials
Salaries

facts.context: Which of the following is not a regular national survey carried out by the British government?
facts.truth: Fashion Sensibility Survey
distractors: Labour Force Survey
General Household Survey
Family Expenditure Survey

facts.context: Which of the following groups or organizations can be identified as having the characteristics of a bureaucracy?
facts.truth: The government of a large city
distractors: An extended or consanguine family, including all the relatives by blood, marriage, or adoption
A book club in which books are chosen by members who take turns alphabetically to lead discussion
The volunteers who gather annually to clean the neighborhood

facts.context: Which of the following best describes the definition of poverty used by the United States government?
facts.truth: It specifies the poverty line at a level set in the 1960s and adjusted since to reflect inflation.
distractors: It includes the value of government services, such as Medicaid or public housing, when setting the poverty line.
It is based on the concept of relative deprivation, and the poverty line changes as the distribution of income changes.
It is based on the concept of lifestyle, and the poverty line varies according to the needs of the individual.

facts.context: The 'new racism' refers to:
facts.truth: a subtler form of prejudice, masked by nationalist pride
distractors: a post-modern deconstruction of racist ideas to reveal their lack of depth
racist practices found in newly emerging areas of social life, such as cyberspace
an anti-fascist movement which challenges nationalist politics

facts.context: Industrial production in the nineteenth century was characterized by:
facts.truth: power-driven machinery and the concentration of workers in large workplaces
distractors: increasing numbers of women in high status positions
cottage industries and homeworkers doing piecework in the domestic sphere
the 'managerial revolution' by which ownership and control became separate

facts.context: Robert and Tanya are married and live with their three children. Their family unit is called
facts.truth: a nuclear family
distractors: a family of orientation
an extended family
a communal family

facts.context: Which of the following is a personal document with closed access?
facts.truth: household account book
distractors: government 'white paper'
confidential medical records
the shares register of a business

facts.context: Bowlby's maternal deprivation thesis claimed that:
facts.truth: children deprived of an early, secure attachment to their mother are prone to suffer physically, intellectually and socially in later life
distractors: mothers who are living in poverty cannot afford to give their children the resources that other children enjoy
'mothering' is a socially constructed activity identified in the narratives of new mothers
deprivation is something children inherit, usually through their mother's side
